topics.title: Probability distributions & hypothesis testing
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Binomial: k successes in n trials, $P = \binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ — defect counts in samples. Geometric: trials until first success — time to first failure. Poisson: events in an interval at rate λ, $P = \lambda^k e^{-\lambda}/k!$ — arrivals, rare defects. Hypothesis testing: H0/H1, choose α (Type I rate), compute the test statistic, compare to z-table (σ known/large n) or t-table (σ estimated, small n); p < α → reject. Type II (β) trades against α via sample size. Thompson-tau: iterative outlier rejection keyed to sample size.
topics.recall_prompt: Match binomial, geometric, and Poisson to their physical situations with formulas, then walk a hypothesis test end-to-end (α, z vs t, p-value) and the Type I/II trade.
key_ideas: Binomial = k of n; geometric = until first; Poisson = count at rate λ
$\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ — the choose bookkeeping
Test: H0, α, statistic, table, decide; p-value = evidence against H0
t-table when σ is estimated from small samples (fat tails); z otherwise
α vs β: false alarm vs missed detection — only sample size buys both
Thompson-tau rejects outliers consistently with sample size (never just delete points)

cards.question: Why use the t-distribution instead of z for a 5-sample tensile test, and what does it cost you?
cards.answer: With σ estimated from 5 points, the estimate itself is noisy — t's fatter tails (4 DOF) account for that extra uncertainty. Cost: wider confidence intervals / larger critical values, i.e., you need a bigger effect to claim significance.

cards.question: Your incoming-inspection test passes a bad lot. Type I or Type II error — and why doesn't tightening α from 0.05 to 0.01 fix it?
cards.answer: Passing a bad lot is failing to reject a false H0 ('lot is good') — Type II (β, missed detection). α only sets the false-alarm rate on good lots; tightening it demands stronger evidence to reject, which raises β at fixed sample size. The trade is rigid: only more samples (or a bigger effect, or less variance) buys both down. Remember: α is chosen, β must be computed — power = 1 − β.

cards.question: Sketch the standard normal and a t-distribution with ~4 degrees of freedom on the same axes, and mark what the difference does to a 95% interval.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Same axes, both curves symmetric and centered at 0
- t-curve: lower peak, visibly fatter tails — crossing above the normal beyond roughly |x| ≈ 1.5–2
- 95% critical values marked: ±1.96 for the normal, ±2.78 for t at 4 DOF — the t interval ~40% wider
- Annotation: the fat tails are the price of estimating σ from few points
- Convergence note: as DOF grow (≳30) the t-curve collapses onto the normal — the distinction matters only for small samples

topics.title: Linear algebra: transforms & solving systems
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Geometric matrices: rotation, reflection, projection, translation (homogeneous coordinates); change of basis $A' = P^{-1}AP$. Solving Ax = b: Gaussian elimination (direct, $O(n^3)$, LU factorization reuses the work for many b's); iterative Jacobi/Gauss-Seidel for large sparse systems (converge for diagonally dominant; G-S uses fresh values, ~2× faster). Overdetermined systems: least squares via normal equations $A^TA\hat{x} = A^Tb$ (or QR for conditioning) — the workhorse of curve fitting.
topics.recall_prompt: Cover the transform zoo and change of basis, direct (GE/LU) vs iterative (Jacobi/Gauss-Seidel) solving and when each wins, and derive least squares.
key_ideas: Homogeneous coordinates let translation join rotation/scale as one matrix
Change of basis: $A' = P^{-1}AP$ — same operator, new coordinates
GE/LU: exact, $O(n^3)$; LU amortizes across multiple right-hand sides
Jacobi/G-S: iterate on sparse systems; diagonal dominance guarantees convergence
Least squares: minimize $\|Ax-b\|^2$ → $A^TA\hat{x} = A^Tb$; QR/SVD when ill-conditioned

cards.question: Your code solves Ax = b for a thousand different b vectors with the same A. Why is factoring once (LU) the right move — and why is forming A⁻¹ explicitly still wrong?
cards.answer: LU pays the $O(n^3)$ elimination cost once; each new b is then two triangular substitutions at $O(n^2)$. Computing $A^{-1}$ costs the same $O(n^3)$ but then destroys sparsity (the inverse of a sparse matrix is generally dense) and amplifies rounding error versus solving directly. Rule: never invert — factor and substitute.

cards.question: When does an iterative solver (Jacobi/Gauss-Seidel) beat Gaussian elimination, and what property of A underwrites convergence?
cards.answer: On large sparse systems (FEA, CFD, circuit nets) where $O(n^3)$ work and fill-in are unaffordable: each sweep costs only the nonzeros, and you can stop at engineering accuracy. Diagonal dominance guarantees convergence (Gauss-Seidel also converges for symmetric positive definite K); Gauss-Seidel uses freshly updated values within a sweep, roughly halving Jacobi's iteration count. Without dominance or SPD structure, iteration can diverge outright.

cards.question: Rotation and scaling are plain matrix multiplies, but translation isn't. What do homogeneous coordinates change to fix that, and what does it buy a robotics or graphics pipeline?
cards.answer: Translation is affine, not linear — it moves the origin, which no n×n matrix can do. Appending a coordinate (w = 1) embeds the space one dimension up, where translation becomes a shear of the w = 1 plane: rotate, scale, AND translate all become one 4×4 multiply. The payoff is composition: a whole kinematic chain collapses into a single matrix product you can invert and interpolate.

topics.title: Eigenvalues, eigenvectors & factorizations
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: $Av = \lambda v$: directions the operator only stretches. Applications everywhere: vibration modes ($K\phi = \omega^2 M\phi$), principal stresses (symmetric → real λ, orthogonal v), stability/phase portraits (eigenvalues of the Jacobian: signs of real parts → node/spiral/saddle), PCA. Numerics: power method finds the dominant λ (repeated multiplication aligns with it); inverse/shifted power targets specific eigenvalues. Factorizations: LU (solving), QR (least squares, eigenvalue iteration), Schur (triangularization), SVD (any matrix: rank, conditioning, pseudo-inverse — the most robust).
topics.recall_prompt: Define the eigenproblem and connect it to modes, principal stresses, and phase portraits; then explain the power method family and when to reach for LU vs QR vs SVD.
key_ideas: $Av = \lambda v$ — invariant directions; symmetric matrices: real λ, orthogonal vectors
Phase portraits: Re(λ) < 0 stable; complex → spiral; opposite signs → saddle
Power method → dominant eigenpair; shift-invert targets interior eigenvalues
LU = solve; QR = least squares + QR-algorithm; SVD = rank/conditioning/pseudo-inverse
Same eigenproblem underlies vibration modes, principal stress, PCA, stability

cards.question: Why does repeatedly multiplying a random vector by A converge to the dominant eigenvector?
cards.answer: Expand the vector in the eigenbasis: each multiplication scales component i by $\lambda_i$. After k steps, ratios go as $(\lambda_i/\lambda_1)^k \to 0$ — the dominant term swamps the rest. Convergence rate is set by $|\lambda_2/\lambda_1|$, hence shifts to accelerate.

cards.question: Eigendecomposition vs SVD — when does eigendecomposition stop being an option, and what does the SVD guarantee that it can't?
cards.answer: Eigendecomposition needs a square matrix, and even then can be defective (too few eigenvectors) or complex. The SVD $A = U\Sigma V^T$ exists for ANY matrix — rectangular data, rank-deficient systems — with orthogonal U, V and real σ ≥ 0, giving rank, condition number ($\sigma_{max}/\sigma_{min}$), and the pseudo-inverse directly. For symmetric positive matrices the two coincide; reach for SVD the moment the matrix is non-square, non-symmetric, or near-singular.

cards.question: Why do principal stresses always come out real, with mutually perpendicular directions — never complex?
cards.answer: The stress tensor is symmetric, and the spectral theorem guarantees every real symmetric matrix has real eigenvalues and a full orthogonal set of eigenvectors. The same theorem is why vibration modes of symmetric K and M are real and orthogonal, and why PCA axes are perpendicular — symmetry is doing the work in all three.

topics.title: Approximation: Taylor, Fourier, Laplace, Lagrangian
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Taylor/Maclaurin: local polynomial from derivatives at a point — linearization ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$) is the basis of small-signal models and error estimates. Fourier: periodic functions as sums of sinusoids — global, frequency-domain decomposition; coefficients via orthogonality. Laplace transform: ODEs → algebra in s, initial conditions included, the transfer-function machine. Lagrangian mechanics: $L = T - U$, Euler-Lagrange $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = 0$ — equations of motion from energy in generalized coordinates, no constraint forces needed.
topics.recall_prompt: Contrast the four tools: Taylor (local), Fourier (periodic/global), Laplace (ODEs+ICs), Lagrangian (EoM from energy) — what each assumes and when it's the right grab.
key_ideas: Taylor: local fit, $f(x+h) \approx f + f'h + \frac{1}{2}f''h^2$ — linearization + truncation error
Fourier: orthogonal sinusoid basis for periodic signals — spectrum view
Laplace: differential equations → algebra; poles/zeros; ICs built in
Lagrangian: pick generalized coordinates, write T − U, crank E-L — constraints vanish
Linearize about equilibrium (Taylor) before frequency analysis (Fourier/Laplace)

cards.question: When does the Lagrangian approach beat Newton for deriving equations of motion?
cards.answer: Systems with constraints and awkward geometry — pendulums on carts, linkages, rolling: pick generalized coordinates that respect constraints, and constraint forces (which do no work) never appear. Newton requires drawing and eliminating them by hand.

cards.question: Laplace vs Fourier — they look like the same integral. Which questions does each answer, and when does using Fourier flatly fail?
cards.answer: Fourier decomposes a signal into sinusoids: it answers 'what is the steady-state response at each frequency?' and needs the signal to be bounded/integrable. Laplace adds a convergence factor ($s = \sigma + j\omega$): it answers 'how does the system evolve from its initial conditions — and is it stable?' — transients, steps, and growing solutions included, with stability read from pole locations. Fourier fails on unstable or one-shot transient problems (the integral diverges or ICs vanish); for a stable system, setting $s = j\omega$ recovers the Fourier picture as a special case.

cards.question: The small-angle pendulum has an amplitude-independent period thanks to sin θ ≈ θ. How big can θ get before the lie matters, and how do you know without solving the full problem?
cards.answer: Check the first neglected Taylor term: $\sin\theta = \theta - \theta^3/6 + \ldots$, so the relative error is ~$\theta^2/6$ — about 1% at 14° (0.24 rad) and 4% at 30°. Physically the restoring force softens, so the real period lengthens with amplitude. That truncation check — bound the next term — is the general discipline behind every linearization.

topics.title: Vector calculus: the big theorems
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Gradient (steepest ascent of a scalar field), divergence (source density of a vector field), curl (local rotation). Conservative field ⟺ curl = 0 ⟺ work path-independent ⟺ F = ∇φ — then line integrals shortcut to endpoint potential differences. Green's theorem (2-D circulation ↔ area integral), Stokes (circulation around a loop = flux of curl through it), Gauss/divergence (flux through closed surface = volume integral of divergence). These convert conservation laws between integral and differential forms — continuity, heat, Maxwell.
topics.recall_prompt: Define grad/div/curl physically, the conservative-field equivalences, and state Green's, Stokes', and the divergence theorem with what each converts between.
key_ideas: ∇φ steepest ascent; ∇·F net outflow per volume; ∇×F local spin
Conservative: curl-free ⟺ path-independent ⟺ potential exists
Stokes: $\oint F\cdot dl = \iint (\nabla\times F)\cdot dA$
Divergence: $\oiint F\cdot dA = \iiint \nabla\cdot F\, dV$
Conservation laws: integral ↔ differential form via these theorems (continuity: $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho V) = 0$)

cards.question: A force field lets you skip the line integral entirely and just difference a potential. What quick test certifies that — and what's the topological catch?
cards.answer: Check $\nabla\times F = 0$: curl-free on a simply connected domain ⟺ $F = \nabla\phi$, so work = φ(end) − φ(start), path-independent — gravity and springs work this way, friction never does. The catch: on a domain with a hole (the vortex field around a wire), curl can vanish everywhere yet loops around the hole still do net work — curl-free is only local; the domain's topology completes the proof.

cards.question: How does the divergence theorem turn 'mass is conserved' from a statement about control volumes into a PDE — and why is the same trick behind heat and Maxwell's equations?
cards.answer: Write the balance on a closed surface: net outflow $\oiint \rho V\cdot dA$ must equal the loss rate inside. The divergence theorem converts the flux to $\iiint \nabla\cdot(\rho V)\,dV$; since the balance holds for EVERY volume, the integrands must match pointwise → $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho V) = 0$. Any conserved quantity (energy, charge) yields its differential law the same way — 'holds for all volumes' is what deletes the integral signs.

cards.question: A field has zero divergence everywhere. What does that guarantee about flux through any closed surface, and which physical fields live under this rule?
cards.answer: Net flux through every closed surface is exactly zero — field lines cannot start or stop, only loop or run to infinity. That's incompressible flow ($\nabla\cdot V = 0$: what flows in must flow out) and magnetism ($\nabla\cdot B = 0$: no monopoles, so flux entering a magnetic circuit leaves it). Practical use: it lets you move a flux calculation from an awkward surface to any easier surface sharing the same boundary.

topics.title: Solving ODEs & classic PDEs
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: First order: separation; linear → integrating factor $e^{\int P dx}$. Linear constant-coefficient: characteristic roots → exponential general solution + undetermined-coefficients particular (GERF); Laplace handles ICs and discontinuous forcing best. Cauchy-Euler ($x^2y'' + ...$): try $y = x^m$. Nonlinear/systems: phase-plane analysis around equilibria (linearize, classify by eigenvalues); singular points need series methods. PDEs by separation of variables: heat equation ($u_t = \alpha u_{xx}$, diffusive decay of modes $e^{-\alpha k^2 t}$), wave equation ($u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$, traveling/standing modes), Laplace (steady state); boundary conditions select the eigenfunctions.
topics.recall_prompt: Build the ODE decision tree (separable, integrating factor, characteristic equation, Laplace, Cauchy-Euler, phase plane), then separate variables on the heat and wave equations and contrast their mode behavior.
key_ideas: Separable → integrate; linear 1st order → integrating factor $e^{\int P}$
Constant-coefficient: characteristic roots (real/complex/repeated) → solution shape
Laplace for ICs, steps, impulses; Cauchy-Euler: $y = x^m$
Phase plane: linearize at equilibria; eigenvalues classify node/spiral/saddle/center
Separation of variables: PDE → ODEs + eigenvalue problem set by BCs
Heat: modes decay as $e^{-\alpha k^2 t}$ (high frequency dies fast); wave: modes oscillate, energy persists

cards.question: Why does the heat equation smooth a jagged initial temperature profile almost instantly, while the wave equation preserves a jagged pulse?
cards.answer: Separated heat modes decay like $e^{-\alpha k^2 t}$ — quadratically faster for higher spatial frequency, so sharp features (high k) vanish first. Wave modes only oscillate ($e^{\pm ikct}$), all frequencies persisting and propagating — the equation is time-reversible, diffusion isn't.

cards.question: Before solving anything, the characteristic roots already tell you the shape of a linear ODE's response. Decode each case: real negative, complex pair, repeated, any root with positive real part.
cards.answer: Roots ARE the system's poles. Real negative → pure exponential decay (overdamped); complex pair $\sigma \pm j\omega$ → oscillation at ω inside an $e^{\sigma t}$ envelope (underdamped if σ < 0); repeated root → the borderline $te^{rt}$ shape (critical damping); any root with positive real part → the solution grows without bound, and no forcing choice can hide it. Damping ratio and natural frequency are just polar coordinates of the root pair.

cards.question: In separation of variables, why do the boundary conditions — not the PDE — decide which sines and cosines appear, and what role does the initial condition play?
cards.answer: Separation reduces the PDE to a spatial eigenvalue problem; the BCs select which eigenfunctions survive (fixed ends → $\sin(n\pi x/L)$, insulated ends → cosines) and quantize the allowed k's. The PDE itself only dictates how each mode evolves in time (decay vs oscillation). The initial condition then sets each mode's amplitude via orthogonality — a Fourier coefficient calculation. Change the BCs and the entire basis changes; change the IC and only the weights do.

topics.title: Numerical methods: root finding & ODE integration
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Root finding: bisection halves a sign-change bracket — guaranteed, but gains one bit per iteration; Newton $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ converges quadratically near the root but needs the derivative and a good start, and diverges where $f' \approx 0$. Production solvers bracket globally, then polish locally (Brent's method spirit). ODE integration: explicit Euler $y_{n+1} = y_n + hf$ is first order (global error ~h); RK4 samples the slope four times per step for fourth order (~h⁴). Step size is set by accuracy OR stability — explicit methods go unstable when h exceeds ~2/|λ| of the fastest eigenvalue. Stiff systems (fast and slow timescales coexisting) enslave explicit steps to a fast mode that's already decayed; implicit methods (backward Euler, BDF) stay stable at physics-sized steps, paying an algebraic solve per step.
topics.recall_prompt: Compare Newton vs bisection (convergence rate, guarantees, failure modes) and Euler vs RK4 (order, cost), then explain stability limits and why stiff systems force implicit methods.
key_ideas: Bisection: guaranteed on a sign-change bracket, linear convergence (1 bit/iteration); Newton: quadratic near the root, but needs $f'$ and can diverge ($f' \to 0$, bad start)
Practice = hybrid: bracket for safety, Newton/secant for speed (Brent)
Explicit Euler error ~h (first order); RK4 ~h⁴ — halving h buys 2× vs 16× accuracy
Stability is separate from accuracy: explicit Euler requires $h \lesssim 2/|\lambda|$ for the fastest decaying mode
Stiff system = wide eigenvalue spread: the dead fast mode sets the explicit step, not the physics you care about
Implicit methods (backward Euler, BDF) are stable for any h on decaying modes — cost is a (often nonlinear) solve per step

cards.question: Newton finds the root in 5 iterations where bisection needs 50 — why would you ever bisect?
cards.answer: Newton's quadratic convergence (digits roughly double per iteration) only operates NEAR the root, needs the derivative, and fails ugly: it shoots to infinity where $f' \approx 0$, cycles, or converges to the wrong root from a bad start. Bisection cannot fail once you've bracketed a sign change — it grinds out exactly one bit per iteration, no derivative, no luck required. Production root-finders (Brent) bracket first and unleash Newton/secant steps only while they behave.

cards.question: Your explicit ODE integration blows up unless the step is absurdly small, yet the solution it eventually produces is smooth and boring. Name the disease and the cure.
cards.answer: Stiffness: the system has a fast mode (big |λ|) that decayed away almost immediately, but explicit stability still demands $h \lesssim 2/|\lambda_{fast}|$ forever — the step is enslaved to a ghost. The cure is an implicit method (backward Euler, BDF): unconditionally stable for decaying modes, so h is set by the accuracy of the slow dynamics you actually care about, at the price of solving an algebraic system each step. Chemical kinetics, stiff circuits, and fine-meshed thermal models all live here.

cards.question: RK4 costs four function evaluations per step to Euler's one. When is that 4× a bargain, and what problem does it NOT fix?
cards.answer: Per unit of accuracy, RK4 wins overwhelmingly on smooth problems: error scales as h⁴ vs h, so matching RK4's accuracy with Euler takes astronomically more steps than 4×. It does not fix stiffness — RK4 is still explicit with a similarly-sized stability region, so the step limit stands — and discontinuous forcing breaks the high order (the error analysis assumes smooth derivatives; integrate up to the discontinuity and restart).

topics.title: Least squares & regression
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: Fitting $Ax \approx b$ by minimizing $\|Ax - b\|^2$ gives the normal equations $A^TA\hat{x} = A^Tb$ — geometrically, $A\hat{x}$ is the projection of the data vector b onto the column space of A, and the residual is perpendicular to it. $R^2$ reports the fraction of variance explained, but it climbs with every added parameter — honesty requires adjusted $R^2$ or held-out validation. Residual plots are the diagnosis: structure (curvature, funnels, time trends) means the model is wrong, not the data noisy. The fit is certified only inside the data's range — polynomial extrapolation in particular goes feral. Numerics: forming $A^TA$ squares the condition number; use QR or SVD when columns are near-collinear.
topics.recall_prompt: Derive least squares two ways (calculus and projection), then walk the honesty checklist: what R² does and doesn't say, what residual plots diagnose, and why extrapolation voids the warranty.
key_ideas: Minimize $\|Ax-b\|^2$ → normal equations $A^TA\hat{x} = A^Tb$; residual orthogonal to every column of A
Geometry: $A\hat{x}$ = projection of b onto col(A) — the fit is the shadow, the residual the perpendicular
$R^2$ rises with EVERY added parameter — use adjusted $R^2$ / validation data to keep it honest
Structured residuals (curvature, funnel, drift) = wrong model, not noisy data
Extrapolation outside the data's range is uncertified — polynomials diverge fastest
Conditioning: $\kappa(A^TA) = \kappa(A)^2$ — near-collinear columns demand QR/SVD

cards.question: Sketch least squares as a projection: the data vector b, the column space of A, the fitted values, and the residual.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- The column space of A drawn as a plane (a subspace through the origin)
- The data vector b as an arrow poking out of that plane
- The fit $A\hat{x}$ as the foot of the perpendicular — b's shadow on the plane
- The residual $r = b - A\hat{x}$ drawn from the plane up to b, with a right angle marked where it meets the plane
- Annotation: that right angle IS the normal equations ($A^Tr = 0$ — residual orthogonal to every column)
- Pythagoras noted: $\|b\|^2 = \|A\hat{x}\|^2 + \|r\|^2$ — the decomposition behind $R^2$
- Validity: linear-in-parameters models only (the 'plane' must be a subspace)

cards.question: A fit reports R² = 0.97. Give two distinct ways it can still be worthless for prediction.
cards.answer: First, overfit: R² rises with every added parameter, so a high-order model can chase noise and score brilliantly in-sample while predicting garbage — adjusted R² or held-out data exposes it. Second, wrong model shape with strong trend: a straight line through gently curving data explains most variance yet is systematically biased everywhere — only the residual plot shows the curvature. R² measures variance explained, not correctness, and says precisely nothing outside the fitted range.

cards.question: Your residual plot funnels — small scatter at low x, large at high x. What assumption broke, and what does it poison?
cards.answer: Homoscedasticity (constant error variance). The coefficient estimates remain unbiased, but ordinary least squares weights every point equally, so the confidence intervals, p-values, and prediction bands are wrong — overconfident exactly where the data is noisiest. Fix with weighted least squares (weights ∝ 1/σ²) or a variance-stabilizing transform like log(y). The plot, not the R², is what caught it.

topics.title: Optimization fundamentals
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content:
Unconstrained: $
abla f = 0$ is necessary; the Hessian classifies min/max/saddle. Gradient descent steps along $-
abla f$ — simple and scalable, but it zig-zags down ill-conditioned (long, narrow) valleys, with convergence governed by the Hessian's condition number. Convexity is the prize: for a convex objective over a convex feasible set, ANY local minimum is global — the only certificate that the answer found is THE answer; everything nonconvex is multistart and hope. Constrained: at an optimum on a constraint surface, the objective gradient must align with the constraint gradient — $
abla f = \lambda
abla g$ (Lagrange) — because any tangential component would be a feasible improving direction. The multiplier λ is the shadow price: improvement per unit of constraint relaxation.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain what convexity certifies and why, how gradient descent behaves in ill-conditioned valleys, and derive the Lagrange condition ∇f = λ∇g as constraint-gradient alignment, including what λ means.
key_ideas: Convex objective + convex feasible set ⟹ local minimum = global — the certificate nonconvex problems never get
$\nabla f = 0$ necessary; Hessian positive definite → min, indefinite → saddle
Gradient descent zig-zags in narrow valleys: convergence degrades with the Hessian's condition number $\lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$
Lagrange: at a constrained optimum $\nabla f = \lambda\nabla g$ — no improving direction remains tangent to the constraint
λ = shadow price: d(optimum)/d(constraint level) — which constraints are worth money to relax
Nonconvex practice: multistart, accept good-enough local optima, or reformulate toward convexity

cards.question: Why does gradient descent zig-zag down a long narrow valley, and what property of the problem sets how bad it gets?
cards.answer: The negative gradient is perpendicular to the local contour — in an elongated valley that points mostly ACROSS the valley, not along it, so successive steps overshoot side to side while creeping toward the bottom. The Hessian's condition number ($\lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$, the contour ellipses' aspect ratio) sets the pain: convergence slows roughly as the ratio grows. Fixes reshape the geometry — preconditioning/variable scaling, momentum, or Newton steps that use curvature.

cards.question: At a constrained optimum, why must ∇f be parallel to ∇g? Argue it from what a non-parallel component would let you do.
cards.answer:
∇g is normal to the constraint surface, so any component of $-
abla f$ tangent to the surface is a direction you can move WITHOUT violating the constraint while still decreasing f — meaning the point wasn't optimal. At the true optimum, every improving direction points off the surface, which is exactly $
abla f = \lambda
abla g$. The multiplier isn't bookkeeping: λ is the rate the optimum improves per unit the constraint is relaxed — the price tag that tells you which requirement to renegotiate.

cards.question: What does certifying a problem as convex buy you that a million random restarts never can?
cards.answer: A proof instead of a sample: convexity makes every local minimum global, so one converged solver run IS the certificate of optimality. Multistart on a nonconvex problem only explores basins — it can report the best minimum found, never that no better one exists. That's why practitioners spend effort reformulating (change of variables, convex relaxation) before reaching for bigger heuristic hammers: the structure is worth more than the compute.

topics.title: Design of experiments
topics.subject: Applied Math
topics.content: A $2^k$ factorial runs every combination of k factors at two levels, estimating all main effects AND interactions from the same runs — and every effect estimate uses ALL the runs (hidden replication). One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) can never see an interaction (the effect of A changing with the level of B) and wastes runs besides. Randomizing run order converts time-ordered drift (tool wear, warm-up, ambient) into honest noise instead of bias; blocking gives known nuisance variables (day, batch, machine) their own stratum — block what you can name, randomize what you can't. Replication separates effect size from experimental error, giving the F-test its denominator. Fractional factorials ($2^{k-p}$) cut runs by confounding effects with high-order interactions — resolution tells you which aliases you've accepted.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain why factorial designs beat one-factor-at-a-time (interactions and hidden replication), what randomization and blocking each protect against, and what a fractional factorial trades for fewer runs.
key_ideas: $2^k$ factorial: all combinations → main effects AND interactions; each estimate averages over all runs (hidden replication)
Interaction = effect of A depends on level of B — OFAT is structurally blind to it
Randomize run order: converts drift and lurking variables into noise instead of bias
Block known nuisances (day, batch, operator); 'block what you can, randomize what you cannot'
Replication estimates pure error — the denominator that makes significance testable
Fractional $2^{k-p}$: fewer runs bought by aliasing; resolution IV+ keeps main effects clear of two-factor interactions

cards.question: OFAT tuned temperature, then time, and declared both 'optimal' — yet the factorial shows that combined setting is poor. What did OFAT miss, mechanically?
cards.answer: The interaction: OFAT measured temperature's effect only at the baseline time, but the temperature effect reverses (or shrinks) at the other time level — crossed lines on an interaction plot. Its 'optimum' is an artifact of the starting corner. A 2×2 factorial estimates the interaction from the same four runs that give both main effects, and each estimate averages all four — better information from fewer runs.

cards.question: Why randomize run order even when you're confident nothing changes over the day?
cards.answer: Because if you run all the low-level trials in the morning and high-level in the afternoon, ANY drift — tool wear, warm-up, ambient shift, operator fatigue — lands directly on top of your factor effect, as bias you cannot remove after the fact. Randomization doesn't eliminate drift; it scatters it into the error term, where it honestly inflates noise and the F-test accounts for it. Drift you can NAME deserves blocking instead — randomization is insurance against the lurking variables you can't.

cards.question: A half-fraction 2^(k−1) design cuts the run count in half — when is that nearly free, and what exactly did you pay?
cards.answer: You paid in aliases: each effect is confounded with a partner effect (set by the design generator), and the runs literally cannot tell aliased pairs apart. It's nearly free when effect sparsity holds — high-order interactions are implausible, so confounding a main effect or 2-factor interaction with a 3-factor one (resolution IV/V) loses nothing real. It bites at low resolution — resolution III aliases main effects with two-factor interactions, resolution IV aliases two-factor interactions with each other — where a 'significant' effect may belong to its alias, and only fold-over or follow-up runs can break the tie.

topics.title: Atomic structure, bonding & periodic trends
topics.subject: Physics & Chemistry
topics.content: Trends from effective nuclear charge and shell count: across a period, Z_eff rises → smaller radius, higher ionization energy, electron affinity, electronegativity; down a group, shells add → all reverse. Bond type from electronegativity difference: covalent (shared, directional) → polar covalent → ionic (transferred); metallic = delocalized electron sea (conductivity, ductility). Molecular shapes from VSEPR (electron-pair repulsion: linear, trigonal, tetrahedral, bent...). Molecular orbital theory: atomic orbitals combine into bonding/antibonding MOs — bond order, paramagnetism (O2); scaled to crystals it becomes band theory: filled valence/empty conduction bands and the gap → metal, semiconductor, insulator.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain the periodic trends from Z_eff, classify bonds by electronegativity difference, get shapes from VSEPR, and bridge MO theory to band theory (why metals conduct, silicon semi-conducts).
key_ideas: Across: Z_eff up → radius down, IE/EA/electronegativity up; down a group: reversed
ΔEN: ~0 covalent → 0.5-1.7 polar → >1.7 ionic; metallic = electron sea
VSEPR: electron domains (incl. lone pairs) repel → geometry
MO: bonding/antibonding combinations; bond order = (b − ab)/2
Band theory: MO limit of ~10²³ atoms; gap size sorts metal/semiconductor/insulator
Wave-particle duality: photons & electrons show both (photoelectric effect quantizes light)

cards.question: Why does silicon conduct better when heated while copper conducts worse?
cards.answer: Silicon: carriers must be thermally excited across the ~1.1 eV band gap — more temperature, exponentially more carriers (dominates). Copper: the band is already half-full of carriers; heating just increases phonon scattering, lowering mobility. Gap vs no gap flips the temperature coefficient.

cards.question: Ionic and metallic crystals are both held together by strong electrostatic attraction. Why do metals bend while salts shatter?
cards.answer: Metallic bonding is nondirectional — a delocalized electron sea rebonds atoms wherever they land, so planes can slide (dislocations glide) and the metal yields. In an ionic crystal, a half-plane slip brings like charges face-to-face: the slip plane turns repulsive, so instead of flowing, the crystal cleaves. Same bond strength, opposite plasticity — bonding TYPE, not bond energy, decides ductile vs brittle.

topics.title: Organic chemistry & reaction kinetics essentials
topics.subject: Physics & Chemistry
topics.content: Functional groups set behavior: alkanes (inert backbone), alkenes (C=C, addition chemistry), alcohols (-OH, H-bonding), amines (-N, basic), esters/ethers (linkages — polyesters, epoxies), aromatics (resonance-stabilized rings). Stereochemistry: Newman projections (conformers), stereoisomers (same bonds, different 3-D). Reaction types: addition (across double bonds — polymerization), substitution, elimination, rearrangement. Kinetics vs equilibrium: rate law $r = k[A]^m[B]^n$ (orders are empirical); Arrhenius $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$ — the universal '10°C doubles the rate' intuition and the basis of accelerated aging; equilibrium constant K from ΔG; Nernst extends to electrochemical potentials ($E = E_0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$); acid-base (proton transfer) vs redox (electron transfer).
topics.recall_prompt: Tour the functional groups and the four reaction types, then the kinetics core: rate laws, Arrhenius (and accelerated testing), equilibrium vs rate, Nernst.
key_ideas: Functional groups predict reactivity; aromatics resonance-stabilized
Addition across C=C is how addition polymers grow (PE, PP, PVC)
Arrhenius $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$ → accelerated life testing and shelf-life math
Rate (kinetics) ≠ extent (thermodynamics/K) — catalysts change only the first
Nernst: $E = E_0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ — concentration shifts cell voltage (batteries, corrosion)
Acid-base = proton transfer; redox = electron transfer (track oxidation states)

cards.question: Your accelerated battery test runs at 60°C instead of 25°C. Using Arrhenius logic with Ea ≈ 0.5 eV, roughly what acceleration factor are you claiming — and what's the catch?
cards.answer: Each ~10°C roughly doubles the rate → ~2^3.5 ≈ 10× faster aging. Catch: Arrhenius extrapolation assumes the SAME mechanism dominates at both temperatures — high-T tests can activate failure modes (e.g., separator/SEI changes) that never occur in service, or miss low-T ones like lithium plating.

cards.question: A catalyst makes a reaction a million times faster yet leaves the final equilibrium mixture untouched. How can both be true?
cards.answer: The catalyst opens a lower-$E_a$ pathway, and Arrhenius says both the forward AND reverse rates rise by the same factor — so $K = k_f/k_r$ is unchanged. Equilibrium position is fixed by ΔG of reaction (thermodynamics), which the catalyst never touches; it only shortens the time to get there. Corollary: no catalyst can rescue a reaction that's thermodynamically uphill.

topics.title: Magnetism in materials
topics.subject: Physics & Chemistry
topics.content: Magnetism comes from electron spin and orbital moments. Diamagnetic: all electrons paired — weak repulsion (everything, water, copper). Paramagnetic: unpaired spins align with field, thermally randomized — weak attraction (Al, O2). Ferromagnetic: exchange interaction locks neighboring spins parallel into domains (Fe, Ni, Co) — strong, hysteretic (remanence/coercivity → permanent magnets vs soft cores), lost above the Curie temperature. Hard vs soft magnetic materials trade coercivity (memory, magnets) against low hysteresis loss (transformer laminations).
topics.recall_prompt: Explain dia-, para-, and ferromagnetism from electron spin, what domains and the Curie temperature are, and the hard vs soft material trade.
key_ideas: Spin + orbital moments are the source; paired electrons cancel (diamagnetic)
Paramagnetic: unpaired spins, field-aligned vs kT — weak, temporary
Ferromagnetic: exchange coupling → domains, hysteresis loop, remanence
Curie temperature: thermal energy beats exchange → ferro becomes para
Hard (high coercivity) = magnets; soft (narrow loop) = cores, low AC loss

cards.question: Transformer cores and permanent magnets are both 'magnetic iron alloys' — why does each need the OPPOSITE hysteresis loop, and what does choosing wrong cost?
cards.answer: A transformer core re-traverses its loop 50/60 times a second, and the loop's enclosed area is energy lost as heat per cycle — so it needs a narrow loop: low coercivity, easy domain-wall motion (soft, e.g. Si-steel laminations). A permanent magnet must hold its state against demagnetizing fields — wide loop, high coercivity and remanence (hard, NdFeB/AlNiCo). Swap them and you get a transformer that cooks itself or a 'magnet' that erases in service.

cards.question: Heating a magnet past its Curie temperature erases it. What is thermal energy actually defeating, and what kind of magnetism survives above Tc?
cards.answer: Ferromagnetism rests on the exchange interaction holding neighboring spins parallel within domains; at Tc, kT beats the exchange coupling and the cooperative order melts. The spins themselves remain — the material becomes an ordinary paramagnet, weakly following applied fields. Cooling back restores the ABILITY to magnetize, not the magnetization: the domains re-form randomly unless a field is applied.

cards.question: Liquid oxygen sticks between magnet poles; liquid nitrogen pours straight through. What does that reveal about their electrons?
cards.answer: O2 carries two unpaired electrons (in antibonding π* orbitals — an MO-theory result a simple Lewis structure misses), making it paramagnetic and field-attracted. N2 has every electron paired, so it's weakly diamagnetic. Unpaired spins are the entire ballgame: pairing cancels the moments.

topics.title: GD&T & dimensional inspection
topics.subject: Design Tools
topics.content: GD&T separates size from geometry: features of size (pins, holes) carry dimensions + tolerances; geometric characteristics control form (flatness, cylindricity), orientation (perpendicularity, angularity), location (position, concentricity), profile, and runout — each in a feature control frame with datums and optional material condition modifiers (MMC gives bonus tolerance and functional gauging). Datum order (primary/secondary/tertiary) defines how the part is constrained for measurement — it should mirror how the part mounts. Surface roughness (Ra) called out separately. Inspection ladder: caliper (~0.02 mm), micrometer (~0.002 mm), dial indicator (comparative/runout), CMM (full 3-D GD&T verification), optical/laser mic (non-contact, soft or moving parts), protractor/sine bar for angles.
topics.recall_prompt: Explain why GD&T exists beyond ± dimensions: the characteristic categories, datum hierarchy, MMC bonus tolerance — and match inspection tools to the tolerances they can actually verify.
key_ideas: ± tolerances can't control form/orientation/location relationships — GD&T can
Categories: form, orientation, location, profile, runout — feature control frames + datums
Datum order = how the part actually mounts; primary contacts first (3 points)
MMC modifier: departure from max material = bonus tolerance; enables go/no-go functional gauges
Tool to tolerance: caliper 10× looser than what it checks; micrometer ~2 μm; CMM for true position; indicator for runout

cards.question: A hole's position tolerance is 0.1 @ MMC. The hole comes in 0.05 larger than its smallest allowed size. What position tolerance does it actually get, and why is that legitimate?
cards.answer: 0.15 — bonus tolerance equal to the departure from MMC. Legitimate because function (a pin must fit through) depends on the combination of size and position: a bigger hole tolerates more location error while still assembling. It's the virtual-condition boundary that's actually being protected.

cards.question: Sketch the complete feature control frame for a hole held at true position ⌀0.2 at MMC relative to datums A, B, C — label every compartment.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- A rectangular frame divided into compartments, attached by leader to the hole's size callout
- First compartment: the position symbol (crosshair-in-circle)
- Second compartment, in order: ⌀ (cylindrical tolerance zone), 0.2 (zone size), circled-M (MMC modifier — bonus tolerance as the hole departs from MMC)
- Then three datum compartments, left to right: A | B | C — primary, secondary, tertiary; order is meaningful, not alphabetical decoration
- Annotation: A constrains 3 points (seats the part), B constrains 2, C constrains 1 — mirroring how the part mounts
- Note: without the ⌀ the zone would be two parallel planes; without the M the tolerance is RFS, no bonus

cards.question: Two inspectors measure the same part to the same drawing and disagree pass/fail — one seated the part on datum B first. Why does datum ORDER change the answer?
cards.answer: Datums constrain degrees of freedom in sequence: the primary takes 3 contact points, secondary 2, tertiary 1. Seating B first tips the part differently on its real, imperfect surfaces, so every measured position shifts relative to the datum reference frame. The order isn't bookkeeping — it must replicate how the part actually mounts in the assembly, and inspection fixtures/CMM alignments must follow it exactly.

topics.title: FEA in practice: connections, mesh, nonlinearity
topics.subject: Design Tools
topics.content: Garbage in, gospel out — the modeling choices ARE the analysis. Connections: bonded (welded — overstiff if misused), frictional/rough (contact nonlinearity), bolt pretension, RBEs/remote points (rigid or distributed load spreading — rigids artificially stiffen), bearing connections (radial-only). Mesh quality: aspect ratio, Jacobian, skewness; refine where gradients live, verify by convergence study (stress, not displacement). Singularities: re-entrant corners and point loads never converge — stress there is meaningless; use fillets or evaluate at distance. Nonlinear switches: large displacement (membrane stiffening, buckling), plasticity, contact — turn on when deflection ~ thickness or stresses pass yield. Tools: ANSYS Mechanical (full control), SimSolid (meshless, fast triage), SKF SimPro/QuickSim for bearing systems.
topics.recall_prompt: List the connection types and what each falsifies if misused, the mesh-quality checklist + convergence discipline, the singularity trap, and when large-displacement must be on.
key_ideas: Bonded everywhere = secretly welded assembly — check load paths against reality
RBE/remote rigid spreads load but adds fake stiffness; deformable distributes
Converge on the stress you care about; refine gradients, not everything
Singularities (sharp corners, point loads) diverge forever — model the fillet or stand off
Large displacement when deflection ~ thickness (stress stiffening, buckling paths)
SimSolid/meshless for triage; ANSYS for the final answer; SimPro for bearing systems

cards.question: Your mesh-refinement study shows stress at a corner climbing 15% per refinement without leveling off. What's happening and what are the two honest responses?
cards.answer: It's a singularity — sharp re-entrant corner has theoretically infinite elastic stress, so refinement chases infinity. Either model the real fillet radius and converge on its true peak, or acknowledge the local field is fictitious and assess by stress at a characteristic distance / nominal + concentration factor.

cards.question: Your bolted-bracket model uses bonded contact at every interface and the deflection matches the hand calc. Why can the stresses near the joints still be badly wrong?
cards.answer: Bonded contact glues entire faying surfaces — they now carry tension, moment, and shear through faces that in reality can separate, slip, and load the bolts in bearing. Global stiffness barely notices (the load path length is similar), but the LOCAL load path is fiction: bolt loads, prying, and bearing stresses are wrong. When joint behavior matters, switch to frictional contact with bolt pretension; keep bonded only for stiffness-level answers far from the joints.

cards.question: Applying a remote load through a rigid connection vs a deformable one — when does 'rigid' lie, and how does the lie show up?
cards.answer: A rigid remote connection forces the attached face to move as a rigid body — fine when the real attached hardware truly is much stiffer than the part. When it isn't, the rigid constraint suppresses the face's natural deformation: artificial stiffness, fake stress concentrations at the connection boundary, and unconservative deflections. Deformable spreads the load while letting the face flex — the default unless you can defend the rigidity physically.

topics.title: Design methodology toolkit
topics.subject: Design Tools
topics.content: Divergence then convergence. Concept generation: morphological chart (decompose into functions × solutions per function, combine), brainstorming disciplines (Six Thinking Hats assigns modes — facts, feelings, risks, optimism, creativity, process). Concept selection: Pugh matrix (score vs a datum concept on weighted criteria — fast, exposes assumptions). Requirements: House of Quality/QFD maps customer needs → engineering characteristics with correlation roof. Strategy/context: SWOT (internal strengths/weaknesses, external opportunities/threats), PESTLE (macro environment), Porter's five forces (industry competition). Use them as conversation structure, not as oracles.
topics.recall_prompt: Place each tool in the design funnel: morphological chart and 7 Hats (diverge), Pugh and QFD (converge/translate), SWOT/PESTLE/Porter (context) — and what each actually outputs.
key_ideas: Morphological chart: functions × candidate solutions → combinatorial concepts
Pugh: weighted criteria vs a datum — relative selection + assumption surfacing
QFD/House of Quality: customer voice → measurable engineering targets, with trade-off roof
Six Hats: separates thinking modes so critique doesn't kill ideation
SWOT/PESTLE/Porter: situational scans — inputs to strategy, not decisions
All are structured conversations; the value is shared, explicit reasoning

cards.question: Why does a Pugh matrix score concepts as +/−/same against a DATUM concept instead of rating each on an absolute scale — what failure mode does that prevent?
cards.answer: People compare far more reliably than they rate: 'better than the reference at serviceability' is defensible; '7/10' is noise wearing a number. Relative scoring prevents false precision and weight-gaming, and the argument over each +/− is the real output — it surfaces hidden assumptions. Re-running with the winner as the new datum checks that the choice is robust, not an artifact of the reference.

cards.question: Your brainstorm dies because every idea gets critiqued the moment it's spoken. Which tool fixes this, and what's the mechanism?
cards.answer: Six Thinking Hats: it separates thinking MODES in time — everyone wears the same hat at once, so green-hat generation happens with black-hat critique explicitly scheduled for later. Premature evaluation kills divergence because people stop offering risky ideas; deferring judgment isn't politeness, it's protecting the search width. The critique still happens — at full strength, just not during ideation.

cards.question: What does the QFD 'roof' add that a plain weighted requirements list cannot — and when does that matter?
cards.answer: The roof is a correlation matrix BETWEEN engineering characteristics: it exposes which targets conflict (stiffer ↔ lighter, quieter ↔ cheaper) and which reinforce each other. A flat list lets you promise all targets simultaneously; the roof forces the trade-offs into the open before geometry exists, while each engineering target stays traceable to the weighted customer need that justifies it. It matters most when different subsystems own conflicting characteristics.

topics.title: Tolerance stacks: RSS & Monte Carlo
topics.subject: Design Tools
topics.content: Worst-case: sum all tolerances — guarantees fit but brutally tight (and expensive) for long chains. RSS: independent, roughly normal contributors → stack σ combines as $\sigma_{stack} = \sqrt{\Sigma\sigma_i^2}$ (tolerances as $\sqrt{\Sigma t_i^2}$ at consistent sigma level) — realistic for ≥4 contributors, accepts a small predicted reject rate. Monte Carlo: sample each dimension from its actual distribution (including non-normal, mean shifts, sorting effects), compute the assembly result thousands of times — handles nonlinear geometry (angles, trig) where linear stacks lie. Always state the assumption: worst case = certainty, RSS = statistical bet, MC = simulated reality.
topics.recall_prompt: Run the same gap three ways — worst case, RSS, Monte Carlo — and explain when each is honest, including why RSS needs independence and MC handles what RSS can't.
key_ideas: Worst case: Σt — certain fit, tightest per-part tolerances
RSS: $\sqrt{\Sigma t_i^2}$ — assumes independent, centered, ~normal; big savings on long chains
RSS lies when processes are mean-shifted or correlated (same fixture, same lot)
Monte Carlo: arbitrary distributions + nonlinear stack functions → predicted reject ppm
Quote results with sigma level and assumption set; tolerance is money

cards.question: A 6-part stack fits fine per RSS but assemblies fail at 8× the predicted rate. The parts measure in-tolerance. What assumptions probably broke?
cards.answer: Independence and centering: parts from the same mold/lot are correlated, and processes run mean-shifted toward one limit (tool wear, deliberate aim) — RSS's cancellation premise collapses. Monte Carlo with measured distributions (or worst-case on the critical few) gives the honest answer.

cards.question: Worst-case vs RSS on a stack of six equal contributors: RSS allows each part ~2.4× more tolerance. Where does that factor come from, and when must you give it back?
cards.answer: Worst-case sums tolerances ($\Sigma t$); RSS adds in quadrature ($\sqrt{\Sigma t^2}$) — for n equal contributors the ratio is $\sqrt{n}$, so six parts buy √6 ≈ 2.4× looser per-part tolerances for the same assembly limit. The discount is a statistical bet that errors are independent, centered, and roughly normal, with a small accepted reject rate. Give it back when the chain is short (n ≤ 3 — too few terms to cancel), contributors are correlated or mean-shifted, or a single failed assembly is unacceptable (safety interfaces): there, worst-case is the honest call.

cards.question: Sketch the assembly-gap distribution for a 5-part stack, marking the RSS limits and the worst-case limits — the picture that explains why RSS buys tolerance.
cards.answer:
A correct sketch shows:
- Horizontal axis: assembly gap; vertical: probability density
- A bell curve centered on the nominal gap (sums of several independent errors go normal — the CLT at work)
- RSS limits at ±√(Σt²), drawn to capture ~±3σ of the bell — small reject slivers shaded outside them (the accepted ppm)
- Worst-case limits further out at ±Σt, with essentially empty tails between them and the RSS limits — that emptiness is the money worst-case wastes
- Failure-mode annotation: a mean shift slides the whole bell sideways toward one limit, and correlation fattens the bell — both quietly inflate the shaded rejects
- Validity note: picture holds only for independent, centered, roughly-normal contributors
